title: First GET Request
description: Import the requests library and send a GET request to https://httpbin.org/get. Print only the status code.
hint: requests.get() returns a Response object.

title: Print Response Body
description: Send a GET request to https://httpbin.org/get and print the response body as text.
hint: Use the .text attribute.

title: Parse JSON Response
description: Request https://httpbin.org/json and convert the response into a Python dictionary. Print the dictionary.
hint: Use response.json().

title: Extract JSON Field
description: Retrieve https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/todos/1 and print only the value of the title key.
hint: JSON objects become Python dictionaries.

title: Check Status Code
description: Send a GET request to https://httpbin.org/get. Print True if the status code is 200, otherwise False.
hint: Compare response.status_code.

title: Print Response Headers
description: Print all headers returned by https://httpbin.org/get.
hint: Headers behave like a dictionary.

title: Send Custom Header
description: Send a GET request to https://httpbin.org/headers with User-Agent set to CodeDojo. Print the JSON response.
hint: Pass custom headers using the headers argument.

title: Send Query Parameter
description: Send a GET request to https://httpbin.org/get with query parameter language=python. Print the JSON response.
hint: Use the params argument.

title: Multiple Query Parameters
description: Send a GET request with query parameters page=2 and limit=50 to https://httpbin.org/get. Print the JSON response.
hint: params accepts a dictionary.

title: Handle Connection Error
description: Attempt to send a GET request to https://invalid.example. Catch any RequestException and print Connection Failed.
solution: import requests; import requests.exceptions; try: requests.get("https://invalid.example"); except requests.exceptions.RequestException: print("Connection Failed")
hint: Wrap network requests inside try/except.

title: Request with Timeout
description: Send a GET request to https://httpbin.org/delay/2 with a timeout of 5 seconds. Print the status code.
hint: Use the timeout parameter.

title: Handle Timeout Exception
description: Send a GET request to https://httpbin.org/delay/5 with a timeout of 1 second. Catch a timeout exception and print Request Timed Out.
solution: import requests; from requests.exceptions import Timeout; try: requests.get("https://httpbin.org/delay/5", timeout=1); except Timeout: print("Request Timed Out")
hint: Catch requests.exceptions.Timeout.

title: Raise for Status
description: Request https://httpbin.org/status/404. Use raise_for_status() and print HTTP Error if an exception occurs.
solution: import requests; from requests.exceptions import HTTPError; try: response=requests.get("https://httpbin.org/status/404"); response.raise_for_status(); except HTTPError: print("HTTP Error")
hint: raise_for_status() raises an exception for 4xx and 5xx responses.

title: Follow Redirect
description: Send a GET request to https://httpbin.org/redirect/1 and print the final URL after the redirect.
hint: Requests follows redirects automatically.

title: Disable Redirects
description: Send a GET request to https://httpbin.org/redirect/1 without following redirects. Print the returned status code.
hint: Use allow_redirects=False.

title: POST JSON Data
description: Send a POST request to https://httpbin.org/post with JSON {"name":"John","age":25} and print the returned JSON.
hint: Use the json= parameter.

title: POST Form Data
description: Send a POST request to https://httpbin.org/post with form fields username=admin and password=secret. Print the returned JSON.
hint: Use data= for form submissions.

title: Basic Authentication
description: Send a GET request to https://httpbin.org/basic-auth/user/pass using username user and password pass. Print the returned JSON.
hint: Pass credentials using the auth argument.

title: Bearer Token Header
description: Send a GET request to https://httpbin.org/bearer with the bearer token abc123. Print the returned JSON.
solution: import requests; headers={"Authorization":"Bearer abc123"}; response=requests.get("https://httpbin.org/bearer", headers=headers); print(response.json())
hint: Authorization headers usually begin with Bearer.

title: Create a GET Helper Function
description: Write a function fetch(url) that sends a GET request to any URL and returns the JSON response. Demonstrate it using https://httpbin.org/get.
solution: import requests; def fetch(url): return requests.get(url).json(); print(fetch("https://httpbin.org/get"))
hint: Return response.json() from the function.

title: Update Resource with PUT
description: Send a PUT request to https://httpbin.org/put with JSON {"name":"Alice","city":"Delhi"}. Print the returned JSON.
hint: PUT replaces an existing resource.

title: Update Resource with PATCH
description: Send a PATCH request to https://httpbin.org/patch updating only the field city="Mumbai". Print the response JSON.
hint: PATCH updates selected fields only.

title: Delete a Resource
description: Send a DELETE request to https://httpbin.org/delete and print the returned JSON.
hint: Use requests.delete().

title: Inspect Cookies
description: Request https://httpbin.org/cookies/set?username=gautam and print the cookies stored in the response object.
hint: Response cookies can be converted to a dictionary.

title: Use a Session
description: Create a Session object and use it to request https://httpbin.org/get. Print the status code.
hint: requests.Session() reuses connections.

title: Session with Default Headers
description: Create a Session with the header User-Agent=CodeDojo. Request https://httpbin.org/headers and print the returned JSON.
hint: Update session headers once instead of passing them every request.

title: Download a Text File
description: Download the robots.txt file from https://httpbin.org/robots.txt and print its contents.
hint: Most text files can be accessed using .text.

title: Save Downloaded File
description: Download https://httpbin.org/robots.txt and save it as robots.txt.
hint: Use Python's open() function.

title: Upload a File
description: Upload a file named sample.txt to https://httpbin.org/post using multipart form-data. Print the returned JSON.
hint: Pass uploaded files using the files parameter.

title: Stream a Large Download
description: Download https://httpbin.org/stream-bytes/10240 using streaming and save it as output.bin.
solution: import requests; response=requests.get("https://httpbin.org/stream-bytes/10240",stream=True); f=open("output.bin","wb"); [f.write(chunk) for chunk in response.iter_content(1024) if chunk]; f.close()
hint: Use stream=True together with iter_content().

title: Disable SSL Verification
description: Send a GET request to https://self-signed.badssl.com/ without verifying the SSL certificate. Print the status code.
hint: The verify parameter controls SSL verification.

title: Check Response Encoding
description: Request https://httpbin.org/encoding/utf8 and print the response encoding.
hint: Every Response object has an encoding attribute.

title: Change Response Encoding
description: Request https://httpbin.org/encoding/utf8, change the encoding to UTF-8 manually, and print the text.
hint: You can manually modify response.encoding.

title: Read API Key from Environment Variable
description: Read the environment variable API_KEY, include it as an x-api-key header, and request https://httpbin.org/headers. Print the JSON response.
starter_code: import requests, os; # Your code here
hint: Never hardcode API keys.

title: Send Request Through Proxy
description: Configure a proxy dictionary and pass it to a GET request to https://httpbin.org/get.
hint: Use the proxies parameter.

title: Retry Failed Requests
description: Create a Session with an HTTPAdapter that retries failed requests three times before failing. Request https://httpbin.org/status/500.
solution: import requests; from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter; from urllib3.util.retry import Retry; retry=Retry(total=3); adapter=HTTPAdapter(max_retries=retry); session=requests.Session(); session.mount("https://",adapter); response=session.get("https://httpbin.org/status/500"); print(response.status_code)
hint: Use Retry together with HTTPAdapter.

title: Configure Connection Pool
description: Create a Session using an HTTPAdapter with a connection pool size of 20. Print the Session object.
solution: import requests; from requests.adapters import HTTPAdapter; adapter=HTTPAdapter(pool_connections=20,pool_maxsize=20); session=requests.Session(); session.mount("https://",adapter); print(session)
hint: HTTPAdapter manages connection pooling.

title: Log Request Details
description: Print the request URL, request headers, response status code, and response headers after requesting https://httpbin.org/get.
solution: import requests; response=requests.get("https://httpbin.org/get"); print(response.request.url); print(response.request.headers); print(response.status_code); print(response.headers)
hint: The original Request object is available through response.request.

title: Create an API Client Class
description: Create a class APIClient that stores a Session object. Implement a method get(url) that returns the JSON response. Demonstrate it using https://httpbin.org/get.
solution: import requests; class APIClient:\n def __init__(self): self.session=requests.Session()\n def get(self,url): return self.session.get(url).json(); client=APIClient(); print(client.get("https://httpbin.org/get"))
hint: Encapsulating requests inside a class improves reusability.

title: Generic Request Wrapper
description: Write a function request_json(method, url, **kwargs) that sends any HTTP request and returns the parsed JSON response. Demonstrate it using a GET request to https://httpbin.org/get.
hint: requests.request() supports every HTTP method.

title: Get All Posts
description: Request https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts and print the total number of posts returned.
hint: The API returns a list of dictionaries.

title: Get User by ID
description: Retrieve user with ID 5 from JSONPlaceholder and print the user's name.
hint: Access a single resource using /users/5.

title: Filter Posts by User
description: Retrieve all posts created by user ID 3 and print the number of posts.
hint: Use query parameters with params=.

title: Create a New Post
description: Create a new post with title Learning Requests, body CodeDojo Practice, and userId 1. Print the returned JSON.
solution: import requests; payload={"title":"Learning Requests","body":"CodeDojo Practice","userId":1}; response=requests.post("https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts",json=payload); print(response.json())
hint: Use json= to send the payload.

title: Update a Post
description: Update post ID 1 by changing its title to Updated Title using a PUT request. Print the response JSON.
hint: PUT replaces the resource.

title: Delete a Post
description: Delete post ID 1 and print the returned status code.
hint: Successful DELETE requests usually return 200 or 204.

title: Get Country Information
description: Request information about India from https://restcountries.com/v3.1/name/india and print the capital city.
hint: The API returns a list of matching countries.

title: List Country Currencies
description: Retrieve information about Japan and print all currency codes used by the country.
hint: The currencies field is a dictionary.

title: Get Current Weather
description: Retrieve the current weather for Delhi (latitude 28.61, longitude 77.20) using the Open-Meteo API and print the current temperature.
hint: Use query parameters for latitude and longitude.

title: Random Dog Image
description: Retrieve a random dog image from https://dog.ceo/api/breeds/image/random and print the image URL.
hint: Most image APIs return the image URL inside the JSON response.

title: Get User Profile
description: Retrieve the GitHub profile for the user torvalds and print the user's name.
hint: GitHub user profiles are available at /users/{username}.

title: Count Public Repositories
description: Retrieve the profile for microsoft and print the number of public repositories.
hint: Look for the public_repos field.

title: List Repository Names
description: Retrieve the first page of repositories for the user psf and print each repository name.
solution: import requests; response=requests.get("https://api.github.com/users/psf/repos"); [print(repo["name"]) for repo in response.json()]
hint: The endpoint returns a list of repositories.

title: Get Repository Information
description: Retrieve information about the repository python/cpython and print its default branch.
hint: Repository details are under /repos/{owner}/{repo}.

title: Print Stargazer Count
description: Retrieve the repository pallets/flask and print the number of stars.
hint: Stars are stored in stargazers_count.

title: Search Python Repositories
description: Search GitHub repositories with the keyword python and print the name of the first repository returned.
hint: Search results are inside the items list.

title: List Contributors
description: Retrieve the contributors for python/cpython and print the login of the first contributor.
hint: Contributors are returned as a list.

title: List Repository Languages
description: Retrieve the programming languages used in pallets/flask and print the language names.
hint: The languages endpoint returns a dictionary.

title: List Recent Commits
description: Retrieve the latest commits for python/cpython and print the SHA of the first commit.
hint: Commits are returned as a list of dictionaries.

title: Paginate Repository Results
description: Retrieve only five repositories for the user psf by using query parameters. Print the number of repositories returned.
hint: GitHub pagination uses the per_page parameter.

title: List Open Issues
description: Retrieve the open issues for microsoft/vscode and print the title of the first issue.
hint: Issues are returned as a list of dictionaries.

title: Count Open Pull Requests
description: Retrieve the pull requests for python/cpython and print the number returned on the first page.
hint: Pull requests have their own endpoint.

title: Get Latest Release
description: Retrieve the latest release of cli/cli and print its tag name.
hint: Use the /releases/latest endpoint.

title: List Repository Branches
description: Retrieve all branches of python/cpython and print the first branch name.
hint: Each branch object contains a name field.

title: List Repository Tags
description: Retrieve the tags for python/cpython and print the first tag name.
hint: Tags are returned as a list.

title: Read Repository Topics
description: Retrieve the repository topics for pallets/flask and print the list of topic names.
hint: Some GitHub endpoints require a custom Accept header.

title: Check API Rate Limit
description: Request the GitHub rate limit endpoint and print the number of remaining requests.
hint: Look inside the rate object.

title: Authenticate with Personal Access Token
description: Read the environment variable GITHUB_TOKEN, send it as a Bearer token, retrieve your GitHub profile (/user), and print your login.
starter_code: import requests, os; # Your code here
hint: Never hardcode personal access tokens.

title: Use Conditional Requests
description: Request the repository python/cpython, extract the ETag response header, then repeat the request using the If-None-Match header and print the second response status code.
hint: Conditional requests help reduce API usage.

title: Build a GitHub Client
description: Create a GitHubClient class that stores a Session object and implements a method get_repo(owner, repo) returning the repository JSON. Demonstrate it using python/cpython.
solution: import requests; class GitHubClient:\n def __init__(self): self.session=requests.Session()\n def get_repo(self,owner,repo): return self.session.get(f"https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}").json(); client=GitHubClient(); print(client.get_repo("python","cpython")["full_name"])
hint: Encapsulate repeated request logic inside a class.

title: Paginate Through Results
description: Retrieve the first two pages of repositories for the user psf (5 repositories per page) and print the total number of repositories collected.
solution: import requests; repos=[]; [repos.extend(requests.get("https://api.github.com/users/psf/repos",params={"page":p,"per_page":5}).json()) for p in (1,2)]; print(len(repos))
hint: Use both page and per_page.

title: Loop Until No Results
description: Continuously request repositories for psf until an empty page is returned. Print the total number of repositories retrieved.
solution: import requests; repos=[]; page=1; while True: data=requests.get("https://api.github.com/users/psf/repos",params={"page":page,"per_page":100}).json(); if not data: break; repos.extend(data); page+=1; print(len(repos))
hint: Stop when the API returns an empty list.

title: Handle Rate Limit (429)
description: Write code that checks if a response has status code 429. If so, print Rate limit exceeded; otherwise print the status code.
solution: import requests; response=requests.get("https://httpbin.org/status/429"); print("Rate limit exceeded" if response.status_code==429 else response.status_code)
hint: Always inspect status_code.

title: Retry with Exponential Backoff
description: Retry a failing request up to 3 times using delays of 1, 2, and 4 seconds before giving up.
solution: import requests,time; delay=1; response=None; for _ in range(3): response=requests.get("https://httpbin.org/status/500"); if response.ok: break; time.sleep(delay); delay*=2; print(response.status_code)
hint: Double the delay after each failed attempt.

title: Fetch Multiple URLs
description: Given a list of JSONPlaceholder post URLs, retrieve each one and print the title.
solution: import requests; urls=["https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1","https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/2"]; [print(requests.get(url).json()["title"]) for url in urls]
hint: Iterate over a list of URLs.

title: Parallel API Requests
description: Use ThreadPoolExecutor to fetch three JSONPlaceholder posts concurrently and print their titles.
solution: import requests; from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor; urls=[f"https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/{i}" for i in range(1,4)]; fetch=lambda u: requests.get(u).json()["title"]; with ThreadPoolExecutor() as ex: [print(t) for t in ex.map(fetch,urls)]
hint: Use ThreadPoolExecutor.map().

title: Parse Nested JSON
description: Retrieve https://api.github.com/repos/python/cpython and print the owner's login.
hint: Access nested dictionaries using chained keys.

title: Cache API Responses
description: Create a dictionary cache so repeated requests to the same URL do not trigger another HTTP request.
solution: import requests; cache={}; def get_json(url):\n if url not in cache: cache[url]=requests.get(url).json();\n return cache[url]; print(get_json("https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1")["id"])
hint: Store responses using the URL as the key.

title: Generic REST Client
description: Build a RestClient class with get(), post(), put(), and delete() methods using a shared Session object.
hint: Centralize common HTTP operations in one class.

title: Build a Reusable API Module
description: Create a reusable API module that stores the base URL in the constructor and automatically prefixes all endpoints. Demonstrate it using JSONPlaceholder.
solution: import requests; class APIClient:\n def __init__(self,base_url): self.base_url=base_url; self.session=requests.Session()\n def get(self,endpoint): return self.session.get(self.base_url+endpoint).json(); client=APIClient("https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com"); print(client.get("/posts/1")["title"])
hint: Store the base URL once and reuse it for every request.

title: Read Zoho Credentials from Streamlit
description: Read the Zoho Client ID, Client Secret and Refresh Token from three Streamlit text inputs and print the Client ID.
solution: import streamlit as st; cid=st.text_input("Client ID"); secret=st.text_input("Client Secret",type="password"); refresh=st.text_input("Refresh Token",type="password"); st.write(cid)
hint: Never hardcode credentials.

title: Generate OAuth Access Token
description: Exchange a refresh token for an access token using Zoho Accounts OAuth. Print the access token.
hint: Zoho OAuth uses form data, not JSON.

title: Build Authorization Header
description: Given an access token, build the Authorization header for future API requests.
hint: Most Zoho APIs expect this exact format.

title: Validate Access Token
description: Request a simple Zoho endpoint using an access token and print the status code.
hint: A 401 usually indicates an expired or invalid token.

title: Refresh Token Automatically
description: Write a function that checks if a request returned 401. If so, generate a new access token and retry the request once.
hint: This pattern appears in many production integrations.

title: Create a Reusable Zoho Session
description: Create a Session object that stores the Authorization header once and reuse it for future requests.
hint: Sessions avoid repeatedly passing headers.

title: Parse Zoho JSON Response
description: Given a Zoho API response containing nested dictionaries, extract a specified field and print it.
hint: Many Zoho APIs nest records inside a data array.

title: Handle Zoho API Errors
description: If the response contains an error key, print the error message; otherwise print Success.
solution: data=response.json(); print(data["error"]["message"] if "error" in data else "Success")
hint: Don't rely only on the status code.

title: Build a ZohoClient Class
description: Create a ZohoClient class that stores credentials, refreshes tokens, and exposes a generic get() method.
hint: Centralize token management in one place.

title: List Workspaces (Mock)
description: Using your ZohoClient, retrieve the list of Zoho Analytics workspaces and print each workspace name.
hint: This prepares you for real Zoho Analytics automation.

title: Exchange Refresh Token
description: Use a Zoho refresh token to obtain a new access token and print it.
hint: Zoho expects form data, not JSON.

title: Build Analytics Headers
description: Build the headers required for Zoho Analytics API calls using an access token and Organization ID.
hint: Nearly every Analytics request uses these headers.

title: Create a Session
description: Create a requests.Session object that automatically sends the Zoho authorization headers.
hint: Sessions prevent repeating headers.

title: Generate Date Batches
description: Given a start date, end date, and batch size in days, generate a list of (start,end) date ranges.
solution: from datetime import datetime,timedelta; ranges=[]; start=datetime.strptime("2026-01-01","%Y-%m-%d"); end=datetime.strptime("2026-01-31","%Y-%m-%d"); step=6;
hint: This mirrors your deletion script.

title: Delete Records by Criteria
description: Build the payload required to delete rows between two dates using a criteria string.
hint: Zoho deletion relies on criteria expressions.

title: Refresh Token Automatically
description: Write a helper function that automatically refreshes the access token when the API returns HTTP 401.
hint: Production integrations should never require manually updating tokens.

title: Import CSV to Zoho Analytics
description: Upload an in-memory CSV (StringIO) to a Zoho Analytics table using multipart form-data.
hint: Your BeautyFort notebook follows this pattern.

title: Build Import Configuration
description: Create the CONFIG JSON specifying importType, autoIdentify, onError, and dateFormat.
hint: Zoho import behaviour is driven by CONFIG.

title: Parse Import Response
description: Parse the JSON response from a successful import and print the number of imported rows.
hint: Learn to safely navigate nested dictionaries.

title: Build a ZohoAnalyticsClient
description: Implement a ZohoAnalyticsClient class that stores credentials, refreshes tokens, and exposes get(), post(), delete(), and import_csv() methods.
hint: This becomes the foundation for every later exercise.

title: Build Analytics Import URL
description: Construct the Zoho Analytics Import API URL given the data center, workspace name, and table name.
hint: Use f-strings to build reusable URLs.

title: Create CONFIG JSON
description: Build a CONFIG dictionary with importType="append", autoIdentify=True, onError="abort" and convert it to JSON.
hint: Zoho expects CONFIG as a JSON string.

title: Create CSV in Memory
description: Convert a pandas DataFrame into an in-memory CSV using StringIO.
solution: import pandas as pd; from io import StringIO; buf=StringIO(); df.to_csv(buf,index=False); print(buf.getvalue())
hint: Avoid writing temporary files.

title: Upload CSV using Multipart Form
description: Upload an in-memory CSV to Zoho Analytics using files= and data=.
hint: Use multipart form-data, not JSON.

title: Import Replace Instead of Append
description: Modify the CONFIG so that imported data replaces the existing table.
hint: Replace is useful for full refresh pipelines.

title: Chunk a Large DataFrame
description: Split a DataFrame into chunks of 5000 rows and store them in a list.
hint: Large imports are usually batched.

title: Upload All Chunks
description: Iterate over each DataFrame chunk, convert it to CSV, and upload it to Zoho Analytics.
solution: for chunk in chunks: buf=StringIO(); chunk.to_csv(buf,index=False); requests.post(URL,headers=headers,data={"CONFIG":config},files={"FILE":("chunk.csv",buf.getvalue())})
hint: This mirrors real ETL pipelines.

title: Validate Import Success
description: Parse the Zoho response and print "Success" only if the API indicates a successful import.
hint: Always inspect API responses instead of assuming success.

title: Retry Failed Imports
description: Retry an import up to three times if the response status code is not 200.
solution: for i in range(3): r=requests.post(...); if r.status_code==200: break; time.sleep(2)
hint: Network failures happen; retries improve reliability.

title: Build an Import Helper
description: Add an import_dataframe(df, table_name) method to your ZohoAnalyticsClient that converts the DataFrame to CSV and uploads it.
hint: Encapsulate repeated import logic in one method.

title: Execute SQL using Zoho Analytics API
description: Execute SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Products using the Zoho Analytics SQL API and print the result.
hint: Zoho Analytics executes SQL through a POST request.

title: Export Table as CSV
description: Download a Zoho Analytics table as CSV and save it to products.csv.
hint: Use response.content for binary downloads.

title: Load Export into pandas
description: Read the exported CSV into a pandas DataFrame and print the number of rows.
hint: Verify exported data before importing elsewhere.

title: Incremental Import
description: Given a DataFrame containing a last_modified column, upload only rows modified after yesterday.
hint: Incremental loads are faster than full refreshes.

title: Delete Rows by Criteria
description: Delete all rows whose supplier equals "BFT" using the Zoho delete API.
hint: Build the criteria string carefully.

title: XML Response to Dictionary

hint: Your supplier scripts rely heavily on XML parsing.

title: Generate Product UUID
description: Generate a UUID for each product if one is missing.
hint: UUIDs uniquely identify imported records.

title: Hash Product Record

hint: Hashes make change detection efficient.

title: Detect Changed Products

title: Build SOAP Envelope

title: Parse XML Namespace
description: Extract product information from XML that uses namespaces.
hint: Namespace dictionaries are common in SOAP APIs.

title: Convert XML Products to DataFrame
description: Build a DataFrame containing SKU, Name, Price, and Quantity from the XML response.
hint: Build a list of dictionaries before creating the DataFrame.

title: Handle Missing XML Fields
description: Replace missing Price or Quantity values with sensible defaults before creating the DataFrame.
hint: Supplier feeds often contain incomplete data.

title: Flatten Nested XML
description: Flatten nested Category → Product XML into one row per product while keeping the category name.
hint: Outer loop over categories, inner loop over products.

title: Validate Supplier Feed
description: Raise an exception if the supplier response contains no products.
solution: if not products: raise ValueError("No products returned")
hint: Fail fast before processing an empty feed.

title: Build Supplier Download Function
description: Create download_supplier_products() that downloads the SOAP response, parses XML, and returns a DataFrame.
hint: Combine the previous nine exercises into one reusable function.

title: Create SupplierClient Class
description: Create a SupplierClient class that stores the base URL, credentials, and a reusable requests.Session.
hint: Use one session for the entire pipeline.

title: Implement Login Method
description: Add a login() method that authenticates with the supplier API and stores the returned token/session.
hint: Store authentication information once.

title: Download Products by Page
description: Download products page by page until the API returns no more data.
solution: while True:\n r=self.session.get(...)\n if not data: break
hint: Stop when the supplier returns an empty page.

title: Merge Paginated Results
description: Combine products from all pages into a single DataFrame.
hint: Collect first, concatenate once.

title: Remove Duplicate Products
description: Remove duplicate products using SKU while keeping the latest record.
hint: SKU should uniquely identify a product.

title: Detect New Products
description: Compare today's feed against yesterday's and return only products whose SKU did not previously exist.
hint: isin() is useful for comparisons.

title: Detect Deleted Products
description: Return products that existed yesterday but are missing from today's supplier feed.
hint: Reverse the comparison from the previous exercise.

title: Build ETL Statistics
description: Generate a summary dictionary containing total, new, updated, deleted, and failed record counts.
hint: ETL jobs should produce summary metrics.

title: Write ETL Log File
description: Append ETL statistics to etl_log.csv with a timestamp after every run.
hint: CSV logs are simple and easy to inspect.

title: Build Full Synchronization Pipeline
description: Create sync_supplier() that authenticates, downloads all products, removes duplicates, detects changes, and returns four DataFrames: new, updated, deleted, and unchanged.
hint: Combine the previous nine exercises into one reusable pipeline.

title: Create ETL Checkpoint
description: Save the last successfully processed page number to checkpoint.json.
hint: Checkpoints let long-running jobs resume after interruptions.

title: Resume from Checkpoint
description: Read checkpoint.json and continue downloading from the saved page instead of page 1.
hint: Default to page 1 if no checkpoint exists.

title: Chunk Large DataFrame
description: Split a DataFrame into batches of 2,000 rows for downstream processing.
hint: Chunking reduces memory usage.

title: Retry Failed Downloads
description: Retry downloading a supplier page up to five times using exponential backoff.
hint: Exponential backoff is better than a fixed delay.

title: Detect Price Changes
description: Compare today's and yesterday's product prices and return only products whose price changed.
hint: Merge on SKU before comparing prices.

title: Detect Stock Changes
description: Return products whose stock quantity changed since the previous synchronization.
hint: Separate stock updates from price updates.

title: Generate Change Report
description: Produce a DataFrame containing SKU, change type, old value, and new value.
hint: A structured report helps with auditing.

title: Validate Required Columns
description: Raise an exception if SKU, Name, Price, or Stock is missing from the supplier feed.
hint: Validate before transforming data.

title: Upload Only Changed Records
description: Upload only new and updated products to Zoho Analytics instead of re-importing the full dataset.
hint: Incremental uploads are much faster than full refreshes.

title: Build Incremental Sync Pipeline
description: Implement incremental_sync() that resumes from checkpoints, downloads data, detects changes, generates a report, uploads only changed records, updates the checkpoint, and returns synchronization statistics.
hint: Combine the previous nine exercises into a production-ready incremental pipeline.

title: Authenticate using Service Account
description: Authenticate with Google Sheets using a service account JSON file and return a gspread client.
hint: Service accounts are commonly used for backend automation.

title: Open Spreadsheet by Name
description: Open a spreadsheet named "Supplier Dashboard" and return the workbook object.
hint: open() uses the spreadsheet title.

title: Read Worksheet into DataFrame
description: Read the first worksheet into a pandas DataFrame.
hint: get_all_records() returns a list of dictionaries.

title: Update Entire Worksheet
description: Replace all data in a worksheet with the contents of a DataFrame.
hint: Send the header row first.

title: Append New Products
description: Append new product rows to the end of an existing worksheet.
hint: append_rows() is more efficient than one row at a time.

title: Batch Update Multiple Cells
description: Update an entire range of cells in one API call instead of individual updates.
hint: Batch operations reduce API requests.

title: Synchronize DataFrame to Sheet
description: Compare a DataFrame with an existing worksheet and append only rows whose SKU doesn't already exist.
hint: Avoid uploading duplicates.

title: Build GoogleSheetsClient
description: Create a reusable GoogleSheetsClient with methods read_sheet(), write_sheet(), and append_rows().
hint: Wrap repetitive logic inside a class.

title: Export Supplier Report
description: Create a worksheet named with today's date and upload today's synchronization report.
hint: Daily reports are useful for auditing.

title: Supplier → Google Sheets Pipeline
description: Implement export_supplier_report(df) that authenticates, creates a dated worksheet if necessary, uploads the DataFrame, freezes the header row, and returns the worksheet URL.
hint: Combine authentication, worksheet creation, formatting, and upload into one workflow.

title: Create BaseAPIClient
description: Create a BaseAPIClient that initializes a requests.Session, stores the base URL, and default headers.
hint: Avoid repeating session setup across clients.

title: Implement GET Wrapper
description: Add a get(endpoint, **kwargs) method that automatically prefixes the base URL.
hint: Centralize request logic.

title: Implement POST Wrapper
description: Add a reusable post() method supporting json, data, and files.
hint: Most APIs need a consistent POST wrapper.

title: Automatic Status Validation
description: Raise an exception automatically whenever a request returns a non-2xx response.
hint: Let callers assume successful responses.

title: Automatic Token Refresh
description: Detect HTTP 401 responses, refresh the OAuth token, update headers, and retry the original request once.
hint: This pattern appears in many OAuth APIs.

title: Centralized Logging
description: Log every request's method, endpoint, status code, and execution time to api.log.
hint: Log both successes and failures.

title: Custom API Exceptions
description: Create custom exceptions such as AuthenticationError, RateLimitError, and SupplierAPIError.
hint: Domain-specific exceptions improve debugging.

title: Configuration Class
description: Create an APIConfig dataclass containing URLs, credentials, timeouts, and retry limits.
hint: Avoid scattering configuration values.

title: Extend BaseAPIClient
description: Refactor SupplierClient and ZohoAnalyticsClient so both inherit from BaseAPIClient.
hint: Reuse common request behavior through inheritance.

title: Build Enterprise Integration SDK
description: Create an EnterpriseSDK that exposes supplier, zoho, and google_sheets clients while sharing configuration, logging, retries, and authentication.
hint: Think of this as the single entry point for all integrations.

title: Create ETLLogger
description: Build an ETLLogger class that writes timestamped INFO, WARNING, and ERROR messages to etl.log.
hint: Use one logger throughout the pipeline.

title: Generate Correlation ID
description: Generate a UUID for each ETL run and attach it to every log entry.
hint: Correlation IDs make debugging much easier.

title: Measure API Execution Time
description: Create a decorator that measures how long an API function takes and logs the duration.
hint: Decorators are ideal for reusable timing logic.

title: Build Retry Decorator
description: Create @retry(max_attempts=3) that retries a function after network failures.
hint: This can later wrap all API calls.

title: Handle Rate Limits
description: Detect HTTP 429 responses and wait for the Retry-After header before retrying.
hint: Many APIs expose the retry delay.

title: Save Failed Records
description: Save records that fail during processing to failed_records.csv.
hint: Failed records should never disappear silently.

title: Process Failed Queue
description: Reload failed_records.csv and retry processing only those records.
hint: Retry only failures instead of rerunning the whole job.

title: Create Pipeline Metrics
description: Return metrics including runtime, API calls, uploaded rows, failed rows, and retry count.
hint: Every ETL run should expose useful statistics.

title: Health Check Function
description: Build health_check() that verifies the supplier API, Zoho Analytics, and Google Sheets are all reachable.
hint: Check dependencies before starting a long ETL run.

title: Build ETLRunner
description: Create an ETLRunner class that performs health checks, runs the supplier synchronization, uploads to Zoho, exports reports to Google Sheets, logs metrics, retries failures, and returns a final execution summary.
hint: This class orchestrates your entire integration pipeline.

title: Download Multiple Suppliers Concurrently
description: Use ThreadPoolExecutor to download products from multiple supplier APIs simultaneously.
hint: Network I/O benefits from multithreading.

title: Merge Supplier DataFrames
description: Merge product DataFrames from multiple suppliers into one DataFrame.
hint: Concatenate once after collecting all results.

title: Standardize Column Names
description: Rename supplier-specific columns to a common schema (SKU, Name, Price, Stock).
hint: Standardization simplifies downstream processing.

title: Resolve Duplicate SKUs
description: Merge products from multiple suppliers, keeping the row with the most recent last_updated timestamp for duplicate SKUs.
hint: Sort before dropping duplicates.

title: Stream Large CSV Export
description: Export a million-row DataFrame to CSV without creating unnecessary copies in memory.
hint: Use chunksize for large datasets.

title: Upload Chunks Concurrently
description: Upload multiple DataFrame chunks to Zoho Analytics in parallel using ThreadPoolExecutor.
hint: Ensure each chunk is uploaded independently.

title: Progress Tracking
description: Display the percentage of completed uploads while processing all chunks.
hint: Track progress after each successful upload.

title: Build Job Summary
description: Return a summary dictionary containing supplier count, products processed, upload time, and failed uploads.
hint: Summaries are useful for dashboards.

title: Synchronize Multiple Suppliers
description: Build sync_all_suppliers() that downloads data from several suppliers, standardizes schemas, merges datasets, removes duplicates, and uploads the final DataFrame to Zoho Analytics.
hint: Treat every supplier as a data source feeding the same warehouse.

title: Multi-Supplier ETL Manager
description: Create a MultiSupplierManager class that manages multiple SupplierClient objects, runs downloads concurrently, aggregates results, uploads to Zoho, exports reports to Google Sheets, and generates execution metrics.
hint: Delegate responsibilities to the individual client classes you built in earlier parts.

title: Build Config Loader
description: Create a ConfigLoader class that reads supplier, Zoho, and Google Sheets configuration from a Python dictionary and validates required keys.
hint: Centralize configuration instead of scattering constants.

title: Pipeline Dependency Graph
description: Design an execution order for Supplier → Transform → Zoho → Google Sheets → Metrics using classes and methods.
hint: Break large workflows into stages.

title: Incremental Snapshot Manager
description: Save today's supplier DataFrame as a snapshot and load yesterday's snapshot for comparison.
hint: Snapshots are useful for change detection.

title: Generic Change Detection Engine
description: Write detect_changes(old_df,new_df,key,columns) that returns inserted, updated, deleted, and unchanged DataFrames.
hint: Make the function reusable across suppliers.

title: Generic Data Validator
description: Create a validator that checks required columns, duplicate keys, null primary keys, and invalid numeric values.
hint: Validation should happen before uploads.

title: Build Import Queue
description: Convert a large DataFrame into an upload queue of 5,000-row batches with metadata like batch number and row count.
hint: Store metadata along with each batch.

title: Batch Executor
description: Execute each queued batch, retry failed batches twice, and collect execution statistics.
hint: Separate queue creation from execution.

title: Build ETL Dashboard Data
description: Generate a dictionary containing runtime, rows processed, rows uploaded, failures, retries, and execution status for use in a Streamlit dashboard.
hint: Think about what an operator would want to see.

title: Full Daily Pipeline
description: Implement run_daily_pipeline() that downloads supplier data, validates it, detects changes, queues uploads, uploads to Zoho, exports a report to Google Sheets, stores snapshots, and returns dashboard metrics.
hint: This combines almost every concept learned so far.

title: Mini Integration Framework
description: Create an IntegrationFramework class that manages configuration, clients, validation, snapshots, logging, metrics, retries, uploads, reporting, and exposes a single run() method.
hint: This is the culmination of Parts 9–19.
